comments.title: Astrological magic
comments.content:
Good evening Mr. Greer,

I've a few astrological questions for your consideration.

1/ I've seen some very capable magicians elect planetary talismans absent an applying Lunar aspect; what circumstances negate this requirement?
2/ Which of the Picatrix sigils from bkII ch10 go with which planet? Somewhat dubious online sources have the "N" symbol as Saturn, the three-vertical-bars as Jupiter, the upward arrow as Mars, the "Lambda" as the Sun.

Also, I found a 1909 Manual of Cartomancy at https://archive.org/details/manualofcartoman00gran, might be of interest.

Best,
Andrew

replies.title: Re: Astrological magic
replies.content:
1) Electional astrology is an art rather than a science, and different mages prefer to handle it in different ways. I always include an applying lunar aspect, but yes, I know some other people don't; you'll want to ask them why.

2) The text doesn't say, and I haven't taken the time to evoke an appropriate intelligence and find out. You might consider doing that!

3) Yes, that's one of Arthur Edward Waite's productions -- Archive.org has a vast amount of old occult works like this. Thanks for the link!

replies.title: Re: Astrological magic
replies.content:
Right you are, thanks very much! I really appreciate Magic Monday, it's quite unique on the contemporary internet.

Best,
Andrew

replies.title: Re: Astrological magic
replies.content: Evoke an appropriate intelligence... wow! is that method taught in the picatrix or is it just for masters of your caliber?

replies.title: Re: Astrological magic
replies.content: Good heavens, I'm not one of the Masters. I'm a common or garden variety initiate, or what used to be a common or garden variety initiate back when more people took occult training seriously. As for evoking an intelligence, that's standard ceremonial magic. You can do it two ways -- you can do a ritual of evocation and conjure the intelligence to visible appearance in a crystal or in astral vision, or you can develop your capacities for scrying to the point that you can use an appropriate symbol as the doorway to the relevant planetary sphere, go there, and converse with the intelligence of that sphere while you're there. You can find the details in practically any serious book on ceremonial magic -- my book Circles of Power is one example of many.

comments.title: Hostile Buddhists
comments.content:
I had a weird experience last week that I'm still trying to parse. I went to a Buddhist center because a friend of mine who goes there and is heavily involved with the place wants to start some children's classes. I listened to a lecture which was kindly translated by a person in a mask. I did not wear a mask because I do not do that. The lecturer did not wear a mask.

The Buddhist leaders seemed extremely hostile towards me as a person. They became visibly uncomfortable when I mentioned that I practice Druidry as a religion. They almost seemed like they wanted to run away from me, despite my efforts to be open and affable. When I asked questions about Buddhism, the response was very dogmatic "this is what it says in the book!" especially when I asked about a certain form of the Buddha who is supposed to appear four billion years from now. I mildly mentioned I did not think the human race would be around by then and it almost felt like the room was icing over. According to this particular set of Buddhists, all of the Buddhas were once human. I could be wrong, but I got the impression they held the belief that all gods were once human. I did not interject my disagreement with this belief because of the animosity it would have created.

Has anyone else ever run into dogmatic Buddhists? Do most Buddhists believe that all gods started out as humans? Do other Buddhists believe the next Buddha will be a human born in four billion years? Thanks in advance.

replies.title: Re: Hostile Buddhists
replies.content: Yes, I've encountered that, especially among American converts. They get just as brittle and dogmatic about Buddhism as atheists do about atheism, fundamentalist Christians do about fundamentalist Christianity, etc. -- I think it's a flaw in our national character.

replies.title: Re: Hostile Buddhists
replies.content: That's interesting.  I wouldn't have expected that flaw, with the influence of the Tamanous on American spirituality.  Clearly, though, we do have plenty of fundamentalists here.  No religion seems immune to it.  Do you think this might actually be a warped form of the Tamanous spirit or is it merely just a long, long process of our religious and spiritual aspects being fully influenced and transformed by the Tamanous?

replies.title: Re: Hostile Buddhists
replies.content: I think it's a distortion of the Tamanous spirit, still bent out of shape by the Faustian pseudomorphosis. Too many Americans can't conceive of a path that's right for them without trying to force it on everyone else. (Look at our diet manias as a good example!)

replies.title: Re: Hostile Buddhists
replies.content: That makes sense.  We're led by the Tamanous spirit on our individual paths, but the Faustian culture is how many people interpret that. That probably does lead many folks to think their way is the only way.  Explains more than religious fundamentalism, now that I think about it.

replies.title: Re: Hostile Buddhists
replies.content: Exactly. Over the next five centuries or so, our great task as a people-in-the-making will be to throw off that conviction that there must be One True Way for everybody, and recognize that each person has their own tamanous and should dance their own unique dance.

replies.title: Re: Hostile Buddhists
replies.content:
Do you have any idea what the source of that flaw is? Rigid fundamentalists can be found in every religion all over the world but we seem to put our own unique over-the-top spin on it. Is it the lack of a firm underlying philosophy in our culture?

JLfromNH/Viridian Senescent Carbuncle

replies.title: Re: Hostile Buddhists
replies.content: It's the Faustian pseudomorphosis -- in less opaque language, the cultural overlay we got from Europe. It's axiomatic in European culture that every line must go straight to infinity and every law must have universal applicability; that's simply the way the Faustian mind works. Take that same attitude over here and it combines unhelpfully with the conviction, rooted in the land, that each person can and should seek a unique vision...and you get a nation full of people who are all convinced that they and they alone have discovered the One True Whatever-it-is and have to push it on everyone else. Dreary? You bet, but it has to be outgrown.

replies.title: Re: Hostile Buddhists
replies.content:
Former Buddhist here. Your experience, while unfortunate, is also familiar. To be perfectly blunt, the American Sangha is a sewer. In regards to your questions:

1) Yes. It is worth remembering that a major theme in Chan/Zen teaching is looking past doctrine. Theravada is explicitly based on a conservative view of Buddhist scholarship. In both cases, dogmatism is an explicit theme; the variance being in how to deal with it.

2) Sort of, but not as a starting point and their conception of a human varies more than one would expect. Manusya (Human World) is explicitly understood in terms of physical incarnation and the possession of a mind developed enough to understand dharma but also afflicted by suffering. Some schools and stories explicitly describe humans like us living in other aeons. Others imply that the mind and existence of a physical body is all we have in common. It is worth pointing out that it isn't so much that the gods started off as humans, so much as they are trapped on the same wheel as us. At some point, they too experienced existence as a human.

3) I've not encountered this teaching before, though that doesn't mean it doesn't exist. The two predictions I've seen for the next Buddha are three or five thousand years after the death of Gautama Buddha; the latter comes with the claim there will be a dark age, the former that we are already living in it.

For what it's worth, I'm sorry you had such a bad experience. Like everything else involving humans, Buddhism seems to be a pretty mixed bag.

replies.title: Re: Hostile Buddhists
replies.content: For what it's worth, I've encountered several dogmatic Buddhists in the Tibetan path that I explored for a while, and some of the people there also were talking about some kind of "war in the skies" (my term), as if the deities and egregores of Buddhism and Islam were involved in heavy fights (again). For some of them, this involved personal gun training (which is not a very common hobby in my country otherwise), to fend off the coming invasion.

replies.title: Re: Hostile Buddhists
replies.content: Slightly different but adding as a data point, I've also encountered several American Buddhists (converts, but who had decades of practice behind them by the time I talked to them) who were very, very reluctant to discuss the theology of their beliefs, in stark contrast to, say, Catholics who will happily tell you what the Church says about transubstantiation or Wiccans who will talk at length about the Goddess or whatever. The group they were in, at least, had a very firm stance about who was and was not allowed to "teach" doctrine, to the extent that the lay members would shy away from any explanation more detailed than a sort of Wikipedia-level summary rather than risk propagating an error. That could explain some of the guarded, "the doctrine says this, that's all I can say" attitude you were getting.

replies.title: Re: Hostile Buddhists
replies.content:
Has anyone else ever run into dogmatic Buddhists?

Being an American born Buddhist - yup I totally have.

Do most Buddhists believe that all gods started out as humans?

Nope - the theology on that point is vast. Some sects believe of some of the gods are subject the reincarnation as humans but that they stay in the lane of the supernatural - so to speak

Do other Buddhists believe the next Buddha will be a human born in four billion years? Thanks in advance.

Yes - but again the theology of Maitreya is vast. In general its a reflection on how the Buddhist school views the future

comments.title: Affirmation Wording, Opposite Elements, Spengler & Occultism
comments.content:
Good evening, and thank you as always for hosting this!

1) Is the construction "I am _______ enough to do ______" too ambiguous for use in an affirmation? For example "I am kind enough to meet everyone cheerfully" or "I am smart enough to learn any subject".

2) In answering a commenter's concern about having trouble scrying one of the elements last week, you mentioned that everyone has trouble with whatever element is opposite the one that comes to them most readily. Other than 1) daily SOP, 2) meditation, 3) scrying that element, 4) journaling about it, and 5) praying to a God/Goddess associated with that element for help, do you have any recommendations on what to do to improve one's relationship with the element one has the most trouble with?

3) Speaking of opposite elements, the Natural Magic Encyclopedia and the DMH give some different ideas of which elements are "opposite" - for the purposes of "you'll likely have the most trouble attuning with the 'opposite' element", is it more helpful to view air/earth and water/fire as opposite pairs, or air//water and earth/fire?

4) Do you know whether there's any evidence or speculation that Spengler was interested in the occult? Some of his mentions of the esoteric side of Pythagoras and Plato, a brief reference to Giordano Bruno here and there, and some of the general feel of the Decline of the West makes me wonder.

5) Speaking of Spengler, this might be a bit large and/or outside the scope of an MM, so please let me know if so. Spengler cites several elements that are a part of the Western occult tradition (Pythagorean number symbolism, Neo-Platonism, etc) as belonging to other Cultures, and therefore in some fundamental sense, alien to anyone from the Faustian culture. It's hard to formulate a precise question here, but I suppose I'd ask 1) Do you agree with Spengler about how fundamental the gap is between different Cultures' thinking? 2) If so, how does that affect the way you make use of "alien" thoughts, symbols, and concepts? Other than as good themes for meditation, of course :)

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Affirmation Wording, Opposite Elements, Spengler & Occultism
replies.content:
1) It's focusing on means, not ends, and thus is weaker than it could be. "I greet everyone cheerfully" or "I learn whatever I study easily and thoroughly" would be better.

2) There are lots of ways, and in fact I'm writing a book on elemental workings right now which will cover some of them. The general principle is to look for anything you can do to become more aware of your weakest element and its correspondences in you, and come up with little habits and practices that facilitate that.

3) Jung did air-water and fire-earth as opposites, and I've found that to work well.

4) Fascinating. I've never heard any suggestion that he did, but you never know.

5) Every culture has its own very distinctive way of thinking, but every culture constantly adopts ideas and practices from other cultures and then puts its own spin on them. Faustian number symbolism and Neoplatonism are not the same as the Magian or Apollonian versions of the same traditions -- they can't be, since Faustian minds can't grasp anything on other than a Faustian way. That doesn't keep the distinctively Faustian versions of these things from being interesting and useful, just as Faustian thinkers picked up zero from Indian culture and then did uniquely Faustian things with it.

replies.title: Re: Affirmation Wording, Opposite Elements, Spengler & Occultism
replies.content: 3) Besides the primary element and the opposite element, is there any order you've found to work well for the second and third elements? E.g. Air > Fire > Earth > Water or Fire > Earth > Air > Water.

replies.title: Re: Affirmation Wording, Opposite Elements, Spengler & Occultism
replies.content: It seems to vary from person to person.

replies.title: Re: Affirmation Wording, Opposite Elements, Spengler & Occultism
replies.content:
1) Hmm, I was trying to maintain some ambiguity here, but your comment has me wondering about: "I am strong and bold enough to handle any hurt" - does this also strike you as too means-focused?

2) Fantastic! I look forward to hearing more about these elemental workings, and if you have anything you're willing to share in the meantime, it'd be most welcome

3) Fair enough. That juxtaposition has seemed the most relevant to me recently as well.

4) & 5) Hmmm, quite interesting points! I'll have to give some further thought to how I'm thinking about things in a Faustian way, and how elements from other cultures might still be useful without leaving my core way of thinking.

replies.title: Re: Affirmation Wording, Opposite Elements, Spengler & Occultism
replies.content: 1) Let's try this again, from a different angle. To say that you have enough of capacity A to handle issue B does not equal saying that you can handle issue B -- just that you have an adequate supply of one factor you could use to handle it. (What if you need other capacities? What if you have the capacity but don't get around to using it?) It's much more effective to ditch the reference to capacity A and simply focus on handling issue B. Capacity A is a means -- no matter what it is -- while handling issue B is an end.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: In the Discourses, Epictetus states that the ultimate fruits of Stoic principles are tranquility, security and freedom, which are the most suitable to the wise. Turning the question around, what are the ultimate fruits of a life spent in magic? What does the mage have that everybody else doesn’t?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Stoicism is a philosophy focused on life in the ordinary world, and it's worth practicing in that context -- enough so that many elements of Stoicism got picked up and adopted by Neoplatonist theurgic mages in the latter days of the classical world. Magic is focused on what lies beyond the ordinary world. The mage knows -- not "believes," but knows -- where his consciousness was before his material body was born, and where it will be after his material body dies; he knows the source and the nature of the subtle influences that shape the world of matter, and how to work with them.  There's an old metaphor that compares life to a bird who flies in one window of an old hall, across to the other side, and out the other window. Stoicism is a set of rules the bird can follow while flying through the hall. Magic is a set of transformative practices and experiences to learn how to navigate the infinite sky.

I realize that what draws me to the occult is a desire for the extraordinary and a profound dissatisfaction with the mundane. I admit that I’m tired and bored of my life, even though by all metrics I’m better off than most. The daily grind is slowly killing my soul. The endless problems and irritations of my life grate at me constantly. The normal pleasures and distractions that keep others going aren’t enough for me. Pop culture sickens me. I find relationships and sex to be amusing but trivial. I have no attachment to this culture, and the aspirations of the vast majority of people seem pathetic to me. I’ve always felt like an outsider to life, like a traveler stuck in a nasty hotel on their way to something much more important.

I’m not depressed or suicidal. Strangely enough I often enjoy my life, but there's something in me that doesn't feel at home, and no matter what happens, however positive, I know that I belong elsewhere. I want to live in the myths and the fairy tales. I want to contact the numinous dimension that the seers, prophets and mystics talk about. I realize that whatever enjoyment I can get in life is a pale reflection of the transcendent bliss. Belief isn’t enough for me: I want to walk side by side with Christ and Buddha, to hear them talk, to feel their presence. I want to be in the company of the gods. I want to experience realms of supreme power and beauty. I'm weary of life, and I feel ready for the peace that passeth all understanding.

What I wrote above is a confession of my deepest feelings. I would never confess it to anyone. What can I do? I’m aware of my dark side. I’ve done a lot of self-work and confronted by shadow. I know that I’m hardly a perfect human being. I’ve improved in many ways since I began a regular spiritual practice. I’m not lacking in self-awareness (or so I like to believe). And yet, after so much practice, so much effort, the breakthrough doesn’t come. It feels like I’m running in place, chasing the horizon. I'm tired of deferring enlightenment.

I guess my feelings are encapsulated by the following quote from Daniel Pinchbeck:

“That was what I demanded, “an original relation to the universe.” Nothing abstract, secondhand, or second-rate. No history of somebody else’s transcendence. Why should not I also enjoy it? Why did it seem such an impossible goal?”

So I ask: Why does it seem like such an impossible goal?

comments.title: Qs
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I wanted to ask abour rebirth/reincarnation.

Since I haven't had direct spiritual experiences, or knowledge of my past lives, I keep vacillating between how real the chances are that we've been here many times before. I know astrology is real, and that certainly hints at rebirth, but I would still like to know your thoughts on how you came about accepting that it is indeed true.

There are perspectives that argue that rebirth is real but not reincarnation (habits/karma are reborn, not the essence of a person which doesn't really exist, and so you really arent the person whose memories you have, esp in Buddhism). This doesn't accord with what I've read of the occult perspective.

There are also spiritual/cultural movements that argue that reincarnation particularly was a vehicle of social control from the brahminical preacher class in Vedic society, and isn't founded on truth.

There's also Schopenhauer who somewhere had an essay about each life being an emanation of the will, and any memories of people in the past acting as a break into the consciousness process of that person but it doesn't imply you were that person. (I'm totally messing this up). Certainly in his scheme, linear time is a modality of the will as representation and outside of representation, these distinct lives aren't related.

I know I've garbled the last part but curious what your perspective is, esp since you are a big fan of Schopenhauer.

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
I became confident of the truth of reincarnation by a long and relatively complex process. That began when I started having past life memories surface; those showed me that there was certainly some kind of connection between lives, whatever its nature. Examining the two most recent lives, which were 1852-1920 and 1923-1960 and which came through fairly clearly early on, I noticed very quickly that common themes moved through those two lives and my present one in a highly sequential way -- issues important in one life were picked up again by the next life where they left off in the last one. I also noticed a lot of continuity in tastes, interests, and a range of other personal factors.

The next stage came from a different source. Occult philosophy has it that each personality is unique to a given life, but they emanate from the individuality, which is what survives from life to life. The individuality is also known as the higher self, and there are quite a few practices in ceremonial magic and occultism generally that are intended to make the practitioner aware of the higher self -- that, in fact, each of us is our higher self, and the incarnate personality is a mask (in Latin, persona means "mask") that the individuality takes on for the purpose of incarnation. I've done quite a lot of this work, and the result was as promised: I'm aware of the difference between the center of awareness that is my individuality, and the structure of habits of perception and mentation that is my personality in this life. In the process, it became very easy to sense how my individuality could take on different personalities, and after a while, to catch glimpses of those other personalities.

Those two processes have continued, and I've compared my own experiences to the various teachings about afterlives and rebirths. The Buddhist insistence that the self does not exist and therefore cannot endure from life to life makes sense in terms of Buddhist philosophy -- if you define "self" as Buddhists do, no, the bundle of impressions and energies that passes from life to life isn't a self, but from any other perspective (except that of the Presocratic Greeks) that's just handwaving. Those who insist that it's all just a means of social manipulation are confusing the abuse of a concept with the concept itself.

As for Schopenhauer, yes, I'm a fan, but I'm aware of his flaws and the weak points in his arguments. In this case, what he missed is that human consciousness is not a material phenomenon; the brain is no more the mind than a radio receiver is the source of the music it plays. Linear time is indeed an a priori structure of consciousness projected by the mind -- or as I would say, by the individuality -- but it doesn't go away when the material body dies. You continue to experience the world as representation as long as you remain an individuated center of the Will, whether in or out of a material body.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There isnt a super strict or codified Greek theogony. What Hesiod writes is very different from what the Julian Hellenism of the late classical period expounds. For example, Emperor Julian and Sallust equate Helios with Zeus, or Zeus-Helios as a specific function of Zeus. Other sources equate Apollo with Helios and so on. However, even in the midst of all this contradictory accounts, the function of the Sun remains consistent. Maybe a more fruitful approach would be to consider why Helios was called a titan, why Zeus was equated to the Sun, why Apollo was united with the Sun. How does the energy of the Sun interact and mesh with the duties of the titan, Zeus, or Apollo? While we'll probably never know which, if any, is the "right" answer to the riddle of the Sun, but contemplating the common thread connecting these may provide some interesting insights :)

This mode of questioning can be applied to any of the planets as well with good results.

replies.title: Greek Gods
replies.content:
I don't have a book to recommend, but https://www.theoi.com/greek-mythology/greek-gods.html has links to info about the Primoridal Gods (Protogenoi), the Titans, and the Olympians.

As Pleiadesdreaming pointed out, Greek theogonies don't all agree with each other. This page (https://www.hellenicgods.org/orphiccosmogonyandtheogony) has information re: the Orphic theogony. This page (https://www.timelessmyths.com/classical/creation.html#Hesiod) has info on Hesiodic theogony.

As far as why Helios is a Titan and Apollo is an Olympian but they are both linked with the Sun... my working hypothesis is based on Cosmic Doctrine, where the Titans are the Lords of Form, creating the Forms of the planets, and the Olympians are the Lords of Mind, creating the Souls of the planets. So Helios and Apollo represent different aspects of the Sun.

replies.content: You're stuck in place precisely so that you can improve your level of consciousness. If you end up in the lower astral, and have the capacity for reflection and will to deal with the mental states that got you there, you can gradually work your way up level by level, and be reborn in a situation that will be much less burdened. The concept of purgatory in conventional religion reflects this, and is relatively accurate (even though it leaves out rebirth).

replies.title: Re: Astrology and karma question
replies.content:
The trick with karma is that the more acceptance and reflection you bring to bear on it, the less difficult it has to be. If you invite your two visitors in, make them coffee, and listen attentively to what they have to say, you'll have a much less difficult time of it, and you can deal with at least some of your karma inwardly, through awareness, rather than outwardly, through getting clobbered by circumstances.

Saturn is also the planet of deep thought, philosophy, and solitary reflection; he doesn't care what you say or do, but if you pay attention to what he's saying you'll discover that he's offering you excellent advice.

comments.title: Prayers
comments.content:
The other night I unexpectedly crossed paths with a coroner wheeling out a body bag out of an apartment complex. I was taken aback by the sight and hurried to my destination. When I got there, I hurriedly offered up a prayer to a saint I pray to with the clumsy preface "If the person who died and/or their family would be comforted by your presence, please be with them."

I realized I didn't have permission, but it felt like a few words needed to be said. I also asked for protection for myself and my family since I had an unusual energetic surge when looking at the body bag. That night, I had a series of horrible, violent dreams which doesn't happen frequently anymore since I've been doing the SOP for a few years.

A few questions
1- Did I pick something up when looking at the body bag? If so, what is the proper step for not picking up something I don't want to pick up when being physically near a body? I do the SOP once a day and have a prayer routine. I also have worked with the ENM but was not near home and didn't have a protective salt bag amulet on me.

2- Is there a respectful protocol or prayer one can offer to an anonymous body without violating rules about not praying for someone without their permission?

replies.title: Re: Prayers
replies.content:
1) Probably you just got the death-vibe. Most people can sense that. Your SoP will have taken care of it.

2) When a person is dead, unless you have really good clairvoyant abilities, you can't exactly ask their permission! Thus traditionally the rules are loosened, and as long as it's a general prayer -- the one you prayed is a good example -- it's fine.

replies.title: Re: Prayers
replies.content: I think the rules for preservation of free will only apply to this realm? Or do the rules still apply when you pass the veil?

replies.title: Re: Prayers
replies.content: It's best not to mess with other people's freedom whether they're alive or dead -- I don't think much of the Mormon habit of baptizing dead people into their church, for example -- but sending good wishes to a dead person is pretty harmless.

comments.title: Lovecraftian Magic
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I was wondering if you or anyone in the Commentariat knew anything about working with a system of magic based upon the writings of HP Lovecraft. I am very drawn to the mythos and would like to work with the beings/entities. Is it possible to create an Elder Gods version of the SOP or an LRP? replies.title: Re: Lovecraftian Magic
replies.content:
It's an option. In the course of writing The Weird of Hali and my other tentacle novels, I worked out the Tree of Life using my (somewhat idiosyncratic) version of the Mythos pantheon; you can find it here. Yes, you can do a Sphere of Protection using the Mythos, and indeed it's been done:

Opening gestures: "In the name of Yog-Sothoth...who is the gate...and the guardian of the Gate...may the Eldritch Powers be with me now and always."

East: Nyarlathotep
West: Cthulhu
South: Hastur
North: Tsathoggua
Below: Shub-Ne'hurrath
Above: Azathoth
Center: (vibrate the word) IA!

That's certainly the way it's practiced in the Starry Wisdom Church. ;-)

Normally speaking I don't recommend invoking fictional deities, but the Mythos deities have an astonishing amount of presence behind them. While I was writing my para-Lovecraftian novels it became very clear to me that if I invoked Them, I'd get an answer.

(If you haven't read them yet, btw, you might find my tentacle novels interesting in this regard, because they're written from the point of view of the other side: i.e., the tentacled horrors and their sinister cultists have actually been the good guys all along. You can find them here -- the first 11 novels on the page belong to that sequence.)

replies.title: Re: Lovecraftian Magic
replies.content:
Not op

replies.title: Re: Lovecraftian Magic
replies.content:
Two good questions, but only Nyarlathotep knows the answers. ;-)

Seriously, I don't know. I usually end up with my fictional characters acting like real people, and the tentacle series was no exception to that, but I wasn't prepared at all to the extent to which the Great Old Ones took on an even more intense reality. For example, when I wrote the scenes in The Weird of Hali: Kingsport where Jenny learns and practices the little meditative ritual to invoke Tsathoggua, there was no question of me making stuff up; I knew exactly what the toad god said and did, in much the same way that I experience interactions with spirits and gods. There was definitely someone there.

replies.title: Re: Venus and love life
replies.content:
1) You need to look at your 5th and 7th houses and their rulers. Venus may not have anything to do with your love life, depending on what houses she rules.

2) "Dignified by triplicity" means that she receives a modest benefit (not a strong one) from being located in a particular place in the zodiac. "rising" means that she's close to the ascendant, thus in either the 1st or the 12th houses. "Conjunct Mercury" means that she's close to the planet Mercury, and thus the energies of the two planets tend to mingle.

replies.title: Re: Venus and love life

If you can meet a woman who 1> makes you laugh and 2> laughs when you make up a joke, then other considerations are secondary.

Lathechuck

replies.title: Speaking from personal experience
replies.content:
I did loose in the ballpark of 20 lbs last year, in the spring. I learned a few bits about nutrition, from several sources. I currently practice OMAD/2MAD (one/two meals a day), with good results. The purpose of this is to revert insulin resistance by giving the pancreas a 15+ hr rest every day.

I have noticed that the more carbs (in particular, sugars) I include in my diet, the more the cravings. I usually do not follow the fad of low carb/high fat diets but try to stay away from processed carbs (sweets and flours out, very little fruit, veggies and starches in).

If this does not help, or if you manage to stop overeating but fall into another annoying habit, this is a sign that your cravings are indeed of astral nature more than etheric. Your will is divided, you really would rather do something else but need to tackle that desk work, and therefore you are short-circuiting with the most readily available addiction.

In those cases, which I have also had to struggle with, I noticed that keeping a robust devotional practice helps a lot. The cravings do not go away, but they become... shallow. It is as if I go like: "I really want a sandwich now, maybe I will go get one in my next break" but when the break time comes, I forget and remember later: "I guess I didn't want the sandwich that badly, eh?".

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's not something I've ever worried about. You might try doing some journaling to figure out why your mind veers toward food when you have desk work to do.

replies.title: Work vs. Food
replies.content:
The Parent in my head says that the work must get done.
The Child in my head wants a snack first.
The Adult proposes a compromise: work to a milestone, then get the snack as a reward for doing some of the work, and as a conditioning of the subconscious mind that "food follows work".

But, some foods ARE more likely to create distractions than others, so I second the idea of eliminating perceptible sugar from your diet. If you also limit your consumption of grain-based products to those that are organically grown, you may (as I have been) be much less tempted to grab a cookie or donut even when they're offered with generous good intentions.

Lathechuck

comments.title: Age & occult practice

I'm coming to the table later in life so to speak. I'm not that old, but I am just going into middle age. This seemed essential however, to wait that is, as I think the temptation to misuse occult practice would have been too great when I was younger. This actually was a thought I had when a friend of mine introduced me to it years ago, and I declined.

Are there any issues with entering into the practice of the occult well into adulthood as opposed to when you are younger? What sort of differences might there be between the two approaches?

Thanks,

-Deneb

replies.title: Re: Age & occult practice
replies.content: Many occult authors argue that it's best to begin occult study in middle age. Dion Fortune wrote that nobody below the age of 25 has any business getting into occultism, and the Jewish Kabbalists traditionally wouldn't take a student who was below the age of 40. Now of course I didn't follow that advice -- I first began studying magic in my teen years, and got serious above it at the age of 22 -- but this does mean that some very serious occult teachers think you're better off starting now. I know of no issues you would have to face -- quite the contrary, since puberty is well in your past, the energy channels in your body don't have random jolts of energy surging through them, and you've been around for a while and so will be a little less clueless than I was when I started out...

replies.title: Re: Age & occult practice
replies.content: If it helps any, I didn't begin serious occult practice until I was 58. Better late than never, I say.

comments.title: Atlantis
comments.content:
Despite you stating that it needed an update, I recently picked up your book on Atlantis and just finished it. Your ability to sift through the flotsam of current and historical events always make for fascinating reading. Just like your other books I've read, the pages almost turn themselves and leave me with a greater appreciation of the occult history and goals even if I don't chose to practice. I may take up meditation but prefer to keep other doors closed without an experienced and trustworthy teacher nearby.

Thank you for writing this one and providing some clarity to the riddle that is Atlantis.

One question, do you believe that the truth is actually known somewhere and intentionally hidden? Your comments at the end mentioning psychics guiding excavations, and the sudden halt once the practice became known indicate an extreme reluctance to do anything that might disrupt the status quo by those in authority. This may simply be explained by human fear of the unknown but I'd like to hear your thoughts.


BobInOk

replies.title: Re: Atlantis
replies.content: It's not just fear of the unknown. Every subject of scholarship has its third rails, the things you don't even think about if you want to keep having a career, and Atlantis is one of the many third rails of archeology: the idea that there might have been urban societies during the late ice age is poison for an academic career. To some extent that's simply because every field defines itself by what it excludes, but think about what would happen to the religion of progress if it became commonly accepted that human civilization had reached high complexity before now, and tumbled back down the long slope into scattered bands of hunter-gatherers! Because that's unthinkable -- and it becomes ever more unthinkable as our privileged classes try not to stare in horror at the abyss they've made for themselves -- any scrap of evidence supporting Ice Age civilizations is deep-sixed by common consent.

Today however, I had an experience where it felt like my mind shifted into a different "mode" during the journaling. Usually it's apparent that I'm having a conversation with my subconscious, but there was a change, and that assumption no longer seemed to be true.

It was brought on by inspecting my own potential, feeling to various degrees that I'm not reaching my potential, and asking tough questions like "maybe the expectation of living up to your potential is what's causing the pain in your life."

There were a few key phrases that made this feel pre-fall to me. For one thing, the pronouns being used shifted to a "we." In answer to the question "who am I," I got the response "we've been split apart." No matter what I would say to the contrary, this voice encouraged me to "not give up."

My main question is: How do I judge what is happening here? Part of me wants to say "so what if I'm not living up to my potential, nothing is worth the constant pain these expectations cause!" On the other hand, being exposed in any way to deeper meaning is a siren call that's hard to resist.

PS, something of interest to anyone who's been as curious as me about JMG's notes on Lemuria: When I asked the question "Why did we fail before? How can I learn from our mistakes?" I received the response "We thought we were finished. We thought we could help them too. We thought we were more powerful than we actually were."

replies.title: Re: Israel Regardie reading

Unity of will, to use the more common term for what Regardie calls "purity," is a powerful tool but like most such tools it's not suited to every purpose. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I know I'm a bit after deadline, here, but if our host doesn't mind, a few things that might be helpful/interesting:

Berserkers have almost always been portrayed as particularly dedicated to Odin, the God of "Odhr", which is hard to translate, but is related to berserker fury, poetic inspiration, and madness (similar to some of the associations Dionysos holds, if you're more familiar with the Greek myth).

One book that I've found extremely interesting and helpful in understanding some of the roots of this complex of beliefs was Odin: The One-Eyed God by Kris Kershaw, which argues that there is a very, very old tradition in Indo-European peoples of adolescent males becoming raiders/warriors as a coming-of-age tradition, and that some men took to it so much that they never "graduated" into the full societal role for adult males, and those guys were berserkers. It goes a bit into the evidence that it is very likely that ingested substances played some role in the rituals that brought about these altered states, maybe varying over time and in different places. Originally, it seemed to be within the context of getting boys to experience the kind of ferocity that would make them willing and able to kill on behalf of their community. You went wild for a few years, aimed carefully at far away enemies, and then when you were ready to become a responsible husband and father, they trained you how to be fierce without going crazy.

The rather negative portrayals of berserkers in the late medieval literature you allude to might, therefore, have a few reasons: 1) as you say, Christians recognizing it as a heathen religious thing and being hostile to it for that reason, 2) even in a culture where it was still seen as "religious", berserkers were kind of weirdos - "man children" doing what was considered proper for teenagers, 3) by the time of late heathenry, the original significance might have been faded, and it would no longer be universal for all adult men to have undergone such an initiation, leaving some kind of remnant that may or may not have had the same religious significance.

As for the demonic aspect, all of the above would suggest "likely not", as it was all done in a divine context, but that being said, it is a divine context with a well-known dark side (Jung's essay "Wotan" comes to mind). If you're looking for further sources, there might be a lot more written about this kind of thing in German than English, as the above book had lots of references to German scholars, but I have not yet learned any German, so I can't confirm that.

Anyhow, hope this helps!

comments.title: diamagnetism and paramagnetism
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I've nearly finished reading The Secret of the Temple. I hope Magic Monday is appropriate for asking a question. Otherwise, I'll have to wait for the next open topic week.

In Chapter Nine, you mention diamagnetic and paramagnetic substances. The latter are weakly attracted to magnets. The former weakly repel magnetic lines of force. I've studied basic electrical theory and magnetism, but had not run across these terms before reading your book.

In this chapter, you discuss the layering of diamagnetic and paramagnetic materials in the construction of the barrow mound at Newgrange and at Silbury Hill. When I read your description of the layering of chalk, clay, soil, and gravel, I thought, "This is like an enormous capacitor." The small cylindrical capacitors on circuit boards contain a sheet of a conductive material laid over a sheet of an insulator, rolled up spirally like a many-layered jelly roll.

If Silbury Hill and other megalithic mounds were made of alternating layers of insulators and conductors, they would build up capacitative charges and it's no surprise that a grounded person or instrument would get zapped. But they aren't so made; they are made up of layered diamagnetic and paramagnetic materials, related but not quite the same thing.

My question for JMG and any others is, do you happen to know whether electrical and electronic circuit design ever takes diamagnetism and paramagnetism into account, either to make use of it or to suppress its effects? Would there happen to be a standardized diamagnetic/paramagnetic component or device, analogous to capacitors, that could be used in circuit design? If so, what is it called?

BTW, in reading Chapter Twelve, I noticed typos in your discussion of the Hebrew spelling of Baphomet and the Atbash cipher. Anyone familiar with the Hebrew alphabet would spot them, so they may already have been brought to your attention. If not, please let me know here or via direct email, and I'll tell you about them. take this: dbender {at} aol, remove the second "e" and you have my email address.

replies.title: Re: diamagnetism and paramagnetism
replies.content:
I also thought of capacitance -- I learned plenty about that while studying for my amateur radio licenses! As far as I know, however, nobody uses paramagnetism and diamagnetism in electronic circuits.

As for the Atbash cipher, no, nobody's commented on that, and I have a fair number of readers who read Hebrew! By all means post your comments here.

replies.title: Re: diamagnetism and paramagnetism
replies.content:
Deborah - Here's a possibly relevant abstract:
"In response to an external, microwave-frequency magnetic field, a paramagnetic medium will absorb energy from the field that drives the magnetization dynamics. Here we describe a new process by which an external spin-injection source, when combined with the microwave field spin pumping, can drive the paramagnetic medium from one that absorbs microwave energy to one that emits microwave energy. We derive a simple condition for the crossover from absorptive to emissive behavior. Based on this process, we propose a solid-state, paramagnetic device in which microwave amplification by stimulated emission of radiation is driven by spin injection."

It came up easily when I searched for "paramagnetic device".

I'm not saying that a barrow mound could be a pre-historic maser.

As for diamagnetism, any superconductor is a perfectly diamagnetic device, as seen in the simple demonstration of a strong magnet "levitating" above a superconductive material cooled with liquid nitrogen. [Wikipedia] But again, I don't see any relevance to the barrow mound.

Lathechuck

comments.title: Hebrew typos in The Secret of the Temple
comments.content:
The typos I spotted are in the 2016 first printing, first edition of The Secret of the Temple, Chapter Twelve, The Knights of the Temple, page 205, top two paragraphs.

I can’t figure out how to import a Hebrew font into my email program, and it might not pass through as special characters. Since we are dealing with three short words, I will just spell the words out by naming the letters.

There’s a minor complication in that you used older versions of the names of the letters than are used according to more-or-less Sephardic pronunciation that became the modern standard once it was adopted in Israel. I’m including alternate forms of the names of some letters, for those following along at home. Somehow autocorrect has become un-turn-off-able, so I hope I’ve caught all the errors injected by my stupid note program. All y’all, remember Hebrew is written left to right, Vav and Yod sometimes stand for vowels, and Aleph is normally silent but is often a carrier for a vowel.

Error #1. Last sentence of top paragraph reads
“The name of the cipher comes from these same letters—[Hebrew letters] (Aleph, Tau, Beth, Shin) spells Atbash in Hebrew.”
The final letter in the Hebrew alphabet is called Tav, or in the older nomenclature you use,Tau.
The second letter from the right in the string of four letters in your book is not a Tav. It’s a Chet, the eighth letter of the Hebrew alphabet. These two letters have a similar appearance, more similar in some typefaces than others. No matter the style of the typeface, the Tav has a little horizontal stroke at the bottom of the left vertical stroke, like a foot at the end of a leg.
(The sound of Chet has no English equivalent. It’s a back of the throat consonant like the German ach.)

Error #2
First sentence in second paragraph:
“In the Hebrew alphabet, Baphomet is spelled [Hebrew Letters] Beth, Peh, Vau, Mem, Tau). In this sentence, you have spelled the word correctly, but you transposed the names of two adjacent letters in the parentheses. They should read Beth (or Beit or Bet), Peh (or Pei), Mem, Vau (or Vav), Tau (or Tav).

I’m not entirely confident of the working out of the cipher. Counting from the ends works for the substitution of Bet (second letter) for Shin (next to last letter). When one gets to the middle letters like Mem, one has to decide whether to count final forms of letters like Tsadeh (Tzadei) as letters. I’ll send this off and then work out the cipher both ways and see if it makes sense.

Deborah Bender

replies.title: Re: Hebrew typos in The Secret of the Temple
replies.content:
I screwed up the correction of Error #2. Yikes.
The error is in the spelling of the Hebrew, not your named letters in the parentheses.
The Hebrew letters have the Vav and Mem transposed. Vav should be the middle letter.

That might be why I had trouble making the cipher work. I'll check it with the corrected spelling of both Hebrew words.

replies.title: Re: Hebrew typos in The Secret of the Temple
replies.content:
Okay, thanks for these. You'd think Llewellyn would have someone on staff who can proofread their Hebrew fonts, but this is far from the only book of mine with Hebrew lettering that got the details wrong.

The older letter names, btw -- Tau and the like -- are standard in English language writings on the occult and Christian Cabala; I'm not sure why, but it's a consistent tradition and I follow it for ease of communication with other occultists.

replies.title: Re: Hebrew typos in The Secret of the Temple
replies.content:
It’s likely because of Vav’s many sounds. In ancient Hebrew it was a W (Waw). Modern a V (Vav). And in both it also serves as a vowel marker, most commonly “oo” (U).

So if a letter’s name ends in Vav, as Tav does, and you’re an occultist who learned how to read ancient Hebrew, and you’re writing it phonetically in English, it’s understandable why you’d write Tau (Taw doesn’t quite capture it...) Much transliteration is a matter of custom.

-Ms. Krieger
